name: El Granado and Bajo Guadiana Greenway Loop
name_original: El Granado – circular por la Vía Verde del Bajo Guadiana
description: Hike the 10.1-mile El Granado and Bajo Guadiana Greenway Loop, a moderate trail through Mediterranean forests and old mining railway history
region_intro: Hiking around El Granado, a municipality in Huelva, Spain, offers diverse landscapes shaped by the Guadiana River and its border location with Portugal. The region features mountainous terrain, riverine scenery, and a network of trails that traverse both natural and historical sites. Hikers can explore paths along the Guadiana River, offering panoramic views and insights into the area's unique geography. Best hiking trails around El Granado The most popular hiking route is Sanlúcar de Guadiana via the Guadiana Natural Path, a 6.3 miles (10.2 km) trail that takes 2 hours 51 minutes to complete. This route follows the natural path along the river, connecting to the nearby town of Sanlúcar de Guadiana. Another top favourite among local hikers is El Granado and Bajo Guadiana Greenway Loop, a moderate 10.1 miles (16.3 km) path. This trail explores the local greenway, offering views of the surrounding countryside and historical elements. Local hikers also love the Viewpoint over the Guadiana River loop from Sanlúcar de Guadiana, a 4.3 miles (6.9 km) trail leading through riverine landscapes, often completed in about 1 hour 52 minutes. Hiking around El Granado is defined by the scenic Guadiana River, varied mountainous terrain, and historical sites such as the Lavajo Menhirs. The network offers options for easy walks and more challenging routes. The routes in El Granado are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 100 reviews. More than 600 hikers have used komoot to explore El Granado's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in El Granado?
A: El Granado offers a wide variety of hiking opportunities, with over 35 trails recorded on komoot. These routes cater to different preferences, ranging from easy strolls to more challenging treks.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes around El Granado?
A: The hiking terrain in El Granado is quite diverse, characterized by mountainous landscapes covering nearly 1000 hectares and riverine scenery along the Guadiana River. You'll find paths that traverse both natural beauty and historical sites, offering varied elevation changes.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in El Granado?
A: El Granado, located in Huelva, Spain, generally experiences mild winters and hot summers. Spring and autumn are typically the most pleasant seasons for hiking, offering comfortable temperatures for exploring the trails along the Guadiana River and the surrounding mountainous areas.

Q: Are there easy hiking options suitable for beginners in El Granado?
A: Yes, El Granado has several easy hiking options perfect for beginners or those looking for a relaxed walk. There are 15 easy routes available, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33804717" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Viewpoint over the Guadiana River loop from Sanlúcar de Guadiana&lt;/a&gt;, which offers gentle terrain and scenic views.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers in El Granado?
A: For experienced trekkers seeking a challenge, El Granado offers 3 difficult routes. One notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1270122" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lavajo Menhirs – Alcoutim Riverside Beach loop from Sanlúcar de Guadiana&lt;/a&gt;, which covers a significant distance and elevation, allowing you to explore historical sites and riverine landscapes.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in El Granado?
A: Yes, many of the trails in El Granado are designed as circular routes, allowing you to start and end at the same point. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984070339/el-granado-and-bajo-guadiana-greenway-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Granado and Bajo Guadiana Greenway Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which provides a comprehensive exploration of the local greenway and countryside.

Q: What historical sites or landmarks can I see while hiking in El Granado?
A: Hiking in El Granado allows you to discover various historical sites. You can encounter the ancient &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5313767" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lavajo Menhirs&lt;/a&gt;, Neolithic stone structures, or explore the nearby border town of Sanlúcar de Guadiana with its &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5301235" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Marcos Castle&lt;/a&gt;. Across the river in Portugal, you can also spot the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6130909" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castelo Velho de Alcoutim&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5597192" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castelo de Alcoutim&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Where can I find scenic viewpoints of the Guadiana River?
A: The Guadiana River is a central feature of the landscape, and several trails offer stunning views. You can find a dedicated &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5301222" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Viewpoint over the Guadiana River&lt;/a&gt; that provides panoramic vistas of the river, the Ratilla riverbank, and the Portuguese side. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4546209" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pontal Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; also offers excellent river views.

Q: Is public transport available to reach hiking trails in El Granado?
A: Information regarding specific public transport links directly to trailheads in El Granado is limited. It is generally recommended to use a car to access many of the hiking starting points in this rural region of Huelva.

Q: Are there parking facilities near the trailheads in El Granado?
A: While specific parking areas for every trailhead are not always signposted, El Granado and nearby villages like Sanlúcar de Guadiana typically offer street parking or small public parking lots where you can leave your vehicle before starting your hike.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in El Granado?
A: The trails in El Granado are highly regarded by the komoot community, boasting an average rating of 4.6 stars from over 170 reviews. Hikers often praise the scenic beauty of the Guadiana River, the varied landscapes, and the historical elements found along the routes.

Q: Are there any long-distance hiking routes that pass through El Granado?
A: Yes, El Granado is part of significant long-distance routes. The Guadiana Natural Trail and the Guadiana Great Route (GR15) both traverse the area, offering extensive paths that delve into the history and natural surroundings of the transboundary Guadiana River basin.

Q: Can I hike with my dog in El Granado?
A: Many trails in El Granado are suitable for hiking with dogs, especially those in natural, less populated areas. However, it's always advisable to keep your dog on a leash, especially near livestock or in protected natural zones, and to check local regulations for specific trails.
tips: Picturesque and quiet town in the interior of Huelva with typical white houses. Among its places of interest is the Solana windmill, the Church of Santa Catalina, the Monument to the Smuggler and a few kilometers away the well-known Vía Verde del Bajo Guadiana.

Windmill restored in 1996 with parts brought from Portugal to maintain its character. It works like those of yesteryear thanks to its reconstruction. Inside you can see various old utensils previously used by millers.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the El Granado and Bajo Guadiana Greenway Loop?
faqs.answer: The trail primarily features a "compacted earth" surface, making it generally accessible. You'll walk through Mediterranean forests and meadows with cork and holm oaks, mastic trees, and kermes oaks. Expect to cross small brooks via bridges and encounter green belts of elm leaf blackberry and reeds. A significant portion runs parallel to the Guadiana River, offering scenic views. You'll also pass through trenches, small bridges, and a 25-meter tunnel carved through rock, remnants of its railway past.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: This loop is generally considered to be of moderate difficulty for hiking, requiring good fitness. However, the "Vía Verde del Guadiana" section is well-conditioned and suitable for walking, often described as having "easily-accessible paths." While some segments might be unpaved, it's manageable for most walkers. It's a great option for families looking for a longer, scenic walk, but be prepared for the moderate length and some varied surfaces.

faqs.question: What interesting sights will I see along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several points of interest. The trail starts and ends in the charming settlement of El Granado. A significant part of the route follows the historic Vía Verde del Bajo Guadiana, an old mining railway. Keep an eye out for the La Solana Windmill, and the picturesque Puente del Lobo (Wolf Bridge), a Roman bridge with three semi-circular arches. The route also features trenches, small bridges, and a 25-meter tunnel carved through rock, showcasing its railway heritage.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: The diverse landscape offers opportunities to spot local wildlife. You might see rabbits, quails, buzzards, and red kites. If you're lucky, you could even catch a glimpse of deer and wild boar, especially in quieter sections.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: The Huelva province generally experiences mild winters and hot summers. To enjoy the trail comfortably, the best times to visit are during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, temperatures are pleasant, and the natural scenery is vibrant. Summers can be very hot, making longer hikes less enjoyable.

faqs.question: Is the El Granado and Bajo Guadiana Greenway Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is generally dog-friendly. However, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash, especially given the potential for wildlife sightings and to respect other trail users. Remember to bring water for your dog, particularly on warmer days, as water sources might be limited.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking in the village of El Granado, which serves as the starting and ending point for this loop. Look for designated parking areas within the village or along its outskirts that provide convenient access to the Vía Verde.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are generally no permits required or entrance fees to hike the El Granado and Bajo Guadiana Greenway Loop or the Vía Verde del Bajo Guadiana itself. It's a public access trail.

faqs.question: Are there any specific regulations or fees for hiking in the Andévalo Occidental area?
faqs.answer: While the trail itself doesn't require permits, it's always good practice to be aware of general regulations when hiking in natural areas like Andévalo Occidental. These typically include staying on marked paths, not disturbing wildlife, and practicing Leave No Trace principles. There are no specific entrance fees for the general area, but always check local signage for any temporary restrictions, especially during fire season.

faqs.question: Does the trail connect with other routes?
faqs.answer: Yes, this loop incorporates sections of several official trails. You'll be hiking along parts of the Vía Verde del Bajo Guadiana, which makes up a significant portion of the route. It also connects with sections of the Camino Natural del Guadiana.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities or places to eat near the trail?
faqs.answer: The village of El Granado, where the loop begins and ends, offers basic amenities. You can find small cafes or restaurants there to refuel before or after your hike. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks for the duration of your walk, as services are limited once you're on the trail itself.
